title: Art, Affect, and Social Media in the 'No Dakota Access Pipeline' Movement
author: Lee

opening_move.description: The viral Standing Rock Facebook 'check-in' campaign (November 2016) appeared to offer digital solidarity but was of no practical value to protesters on the ground. Lee uses this as the entry point for a wider argument about the relationship between digital and embodied activism: rather than dichotomizing online (ineffective) vs. offline (real) activism, Lee argues through Clough's biomediated bodies and Massumi's affect theory that the two are constitutively linked.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Reading the #NoDAPL movement and Cannupa Hanska Luger's Mirror Shields Project through affect theory and the concept of alterlife shows how digital and embodied activism are co-constitutive, and how Indigenous art-activism resists biopower by creating new possibilities for collective life and resistance.

rivals_engaged: dichotomous embodied vs. digital activism frameworks
instrumentalist accounts of social media activism (check-ins as either effective or not)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper proposes affect theory (Clough's biomediated bodies, Massumi's ontopower, Bennett's vital matter) as the framework for understanding how digital and embodied activism are constitutively linked. Luger's Mirror Shields Project is the key case: it mobilizes affect in ways that move beyond both slacktivism and direct action by creating what the paper calls 'alterlife' — new possibilities for collective Indigenous existence.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Analysis of the #NoDAPL Facebook check-in campaign; close analysis of Cannupa Hanska Luger's Mirror Shields Project as an art-activist intervention; contextualization through the history of the Standing Rock Sioux resistance.
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Affect theory (Clough, Massumi); biopower/biopolitics (Foucault via Massumi's ontopower); Indigenous studies; digital activism theory; art criticism.
epistemic_mode.notes: The Mirror Shields Project is the constitutive interpretive case through which the affect theory argument is developed. The #NoDAPL Facebook check-in serves as the opening puzzle. The paper is a normative-with-interpretive-case analysis where art-activism (Luger) illustrates the theoretical claim about biomediated bodies and alterlife.

case_type.case_name: Cannupa Hanska Luger's Mirror Shields Project (#NoDAPL / Standing Rock, 2016)

normative_stake.domain: Indigenous studies, digital activism, affect theory, biopolitics

confidence_notes: The reflexive_move is present: the paper explicitly flags the risk of reading Indigenous worldviews (Mni Wiconi) through posthuman theoretical lens (Todd, 2016), acknowledging that Indigenous concepts predate and exceed posthuman theory. This meta-reflexive qualification is built into the argument. The Mirror Shields Project is the constitutive case through which the affect/alterlife framework is demonstrated.
